title: Borrowed Suppers
passage: Few predators keep a schedule as exact as the ashen pygmy owl of the Cauca highlands. In a three-year survey for the popular-science monthly Field & Frequency, ecologist Marisol Venn tracked forty nesting pairs and found that the owls begin hunting almost precisely eleven minutes after the local bats leave their roosts each evening. The timing is no accident. The bats, sweeping the air first, flush up dense clouds of night-flying moths, and the owls simply harvest the insects the bats shake loose. Venn calls the arrangement eavesdropping on someone else's dinner bell. Crucially, the owls do not chase the bats through the trees; they wait at fixed perches along the ridgelines, letting the disturbed moths rise to meet them. When Venn's team delayed the bats' departure with dimmed artificial light, the owls' first strikes slipped later by the very same interval.

questions.prompt: According to the text, what prompts the pygmy owls to begin hunting each evening?

options.text: A nightly drop in temperature that lifts the moths up toward the ridgeline perches.
Fading daylight alone, since the owls hunt strictly by a fixed internal clock.
The evening departure of the local bats, which stirs up the moths the owls eat.
The owls' own pursuit of the bats through the trees in order to locate the insects.

questions.rationale: TYPE-001 direct detail. Text: owls begin ~11 minutes after bats leave the roost, and the bats stir up the moths the owls then harvest; the light-delay experiment shows the trigger is the bats, not a clock. C paraphrases that. A = outside_knowledge (temperature is never mentioned as the cause). B = quantifier/scope error contradicted by the delay experiment (the trigger is the bats, so it cannot be a fixed internal clock or daylight alone). D = wrong-detail contradiction: the text states the owls do NOT chase the bats through the trees.
